content:
First, observe that since each \(a_i\) lies between 2.56 and 2.86, any optimal integer approximation \(A_i\) must be either 2 or 3.  If we set exactly \(k\) of the \(A_i\)’s equal to 3 and the other \(7-k\) equal to 2, the total is
\[
3k + 2(7-k) \;=\; 14 + k,
\]
and we need this to be 19, so \(k=5\).  Thus exactly five of the \(A_i\)’s are 3 and two are 2.

We now choose which two to round down to 2 so as to minimize the worst error.  For each \(a_i\) the errors are

\[
\text{if }A_i=2:\quad e_{2,i}=|2-a_i|=a_i-2,
\]
\[
\text{if }A_i=3:\quad e_{3,i}=|3-a_i|=3-a_i.
\]
Compute these:

i\quad a_i\quad a_i–2\quad 3–a_i  
1\quad 2.56\quad 0.56\quad 0.44  
2\quad 2.61\quad 0.61\quad 0.39  
3\quad 2.65\quad 0.65\quad 0.35  
4\quad 2.71\quad 0.71\quad 0.29  
5\quad 2.79\quad 0.79\quad 0.21  
6\quad 2.82\quad 0.82\quad 0.18  
7\quad 2.86\quad 0.86\quad 0.14  

To keep the maximum error as small as possible, we must pick the two smallest \(a_i\) to round down (since that keeps \(a_i-2\) small) and round all the others up.  Hence we set
\[
A_1=A_2=2,\quad A_3=A_4=A_5=A_6=A_7=3.
\]
Then the largest “down‐rounding’’ error is \(\max\{0.56,0.61\}=0.61\), and the largest “up‐rounding’’ error among the fives is \(0.35\), so overall
\[
M=\max_i|A_i-a_i|=0.61.
\]
Thus
\[
100M=100\cdot0.61=\boxed{61}.
\]

\]
\[

Compute these:

i\quad a_i\quad a_i–2\quad 3–a_i  
1\quad 2.56\quad 0.56\quad 0.44  
2\quad 2.61\quad 0.61\quad 0.39  
3\quad 2.65\quad 0.65\quad 0.35  
4\quad 2.71\quad 0.71\quad 0.29  
5\quad 2.79\quad 0.79\quad 0.21

7\quad 2.86\quad 0.86\quad 0.14
